applied_paragraph: For Example Health App and the Brain wiki pipeline, this post reframes latency ownership: slow Gemini API calls are not 'infra noise' to absorb — they are a product experience problem. If the enrichment pipeline takes 40 seconds, the owner stops using it. Owning that number as a product metric, not a backend detail, changes what you optimize.

applied_bullets.claim: Fan-out depth multiplies tail latency problems
applied_bullets.why_it_matters: The enrichment pipeline fans out to 3 Gemini calls per post — if any one is slow, the whole run feels slow
applied_bullets.action: Consider parallelizing Pass B and Pass C calls; they do not depend on each other

applied_bullets.claim: Monitoring mean throughput hides the tail
applied_bullets.why_it_matters: A single slow Gemini call that takes 30s will look fine in average metrics
applied_bullets.action: Log per-call latency with percentile breakdowns, not just total run time

applied_bullets.claim: Only production traffic reveals true latency distribution
applied_bullets.why_it_matters: The fixture-based integration tests run under ideal conditions — they will not surface real API latency behavior
applied_bullets.action: Run the pipeline against a real post monthly to track actual Gemini latency trends

socratic_questions: Is there a fan-out in the Brain wiki pipeline that compounds tail latency in ways the integration tests cannot detect?
What would it mean to own tail latency of Gemini calls as a product metric — what SLO would you set?
If the enrichment pipeline felt 'broken' to you, would you know whether the cause was p99 Gemini latency or a logical bug?
